posts.title: Faith and Resilience
posts.author: ELGhazi
posts.excerpt: In the quiet moments between hardship and hope, faith reveals itself not as an escape, but as a foundation.
posts.content:
In the quiet moments between hardship and hope, faith reveals itself not as an escape, but as a foundation. There is a particular kind of strength that belongs only to those who have learned to carry their grief with grace — who have walked through fire and emerged not unscathed, but unbroken.

The olive tree does not grow tall and proud in soft soil. It takes root in stone. It bends under the weight of centuries and still bears fruit. There is something profoundly human in that image — in the refusal to stop bearing fruit even when the ground offers nothing but resistance.

Resilience is not the absence of pain. It is the decision, made quietly and often alone, to remain present. To remain kind. To remain open to beauty even when beauty seems like a luxury you cannot afford.

Faith, in its truest form, is not certainty. It is the courage to continue walking even when the path is not visible. It is the whispered prayer at three in the morning. It is the hand extended to a stranger. It is the choice to see the sacred in the ordinary — in bread broken and shared, in water offered freely, in the warmth of sunlight on ancient stone.

We were not promised easy lives. We were promised that we would not walk alone. That is the covenant — not comfort, but companionship. Not safety, but solidarity. And in that promise lives the deepest peace.

posts.title: The Stone Remembers
posts.author: ELGhazi
posts.excerpt: Every stone in Jerusalem carries the memory of hands that laid it — hands that prayed, that wept, that built toward heaven.
posts.content:
Every stone in Jerusalem carries the memory of hands that laid it — hands that prayed, that wept, that built toward heaven. When you press your palm against those ancient walls, you are not merely touching rock. You are touching time itself.

There is a wisdom in old stones that we have largely forgotten how to hear. They do not speak in arguments or declarations. They speak in texture. In silence. In the way light moves across their surface at different hours of the day — warm and golden at dawn, fierce and white at noon, tender and amber at dusk.

Architecture, at its most sacred, is frozen prayer. The arch does not merely span a doorway — it frames a threshold between the ordinary world and the possibility of the divine. The dome does not merely cover a space — it reaches upward, pulling the eye and the spirit toward something larger than the self.

The Dome of the Rock stands not as a monument to power, but as a monument to the human longing to make the holy visible. To say: here, in this place, something sacred happened. Here, we paused. Here, we looked up.

We need such places. Not because God is more present there than elsewhere — perhaps God is equally present in a refugee tent, in a hospital corridor, in the cramped room where a family sits together and finds reasons to laugh. But because we are creatures who need reminders. We need beauty to help us remember what we are reaching for.

Let us be builders of such reminders — not only in stone, but in every act of dignity, every gesture of compassion, every moment when we choose to treat another human being as sacred ground.

posts.title: What the Olive Tree Knows
posts.author: ELGhazi
posts.excerpt: Some trees live for thousands of years. They have watched empires rise and fall and remained, quietly, themselves.
posts.content:
Some trees live for thousands of years. They have watched empires rise and fall and remained, quietly, themselves. The olive tree is one of these witnesses — gnarled and silver-leaved, rooted in terraced hillsides, patient in a way that puts human impatience to shame.

There is a grove in Gethsemane where some trees may have stood since before the first century. Their trunks are twisted with age, their bark like the face of an elder who has seen too much and chosen, still, to give. They continue to flower. They continue to offer oil — that ancient gift of light and healing and sustenance.

We speak so often of growth as if it were vertical — upward, outward, visible. But the olive tree teaches a different geometry. Its roots go down before anything goes up. Its growth is mostly invisible, mostly underground, mostly patient preparation for a flourishing that may not come for decades.

How many of us are in seasons of rootedness — seasons that feel like stagnation but are actually preparation? How many of us are being asked to grow downward before we are allowed to grow up?

The fruit of the olive is bitter before it is pressed. It must be transformed to become its truest self. There is something in that process — crushing and pressing and waiting — that speaks to every human experience of suffering and emergence.

What we carry may be heavy. What we endure may seem purposeless. But the olive tree does not ask the purpose of rain. It simply opens.

posts.title: On Human Dignity
posts.author: ELGhazi
posts.excerpt: Dignity is not earned. It is not granted by governments or rescinded by poverty. It is the irreducible core of what it means to be human.
posts.content:
Dignity is not earned. It is not granted by governments or rescinded by poverty. It is the irreducible core of what it means to be human — the part that cannot be taken away, only obscured, only buried under weight that does not belong to it.

Every tradition that has grappled seriously with what it means to live a good life has arrived at some version of this truth. In Islamic thought, we speak of karama — a God-given honor that every person carries simply by virtue of being human. In other traditions, different words hold the same meaning: imago dei, buddha-nature, the spark of the divine.

The language varies. The insight is the same: you are not nothing. You cannot be made into nothing. The part of you that is most fundamentally you is untouchable by circumstance.

This is not a comfortable philosophy. If we truly believed it — if we lived as if every human being we encountered carried irreducible dignity — our world would look radically different. We would not look away from suffering so easily. We would not build systems that treat people as interchangeable units of labor or burden.

But this is also a deeply consoling philosophy. Because it means that dignity does not depend on your circumstances being recognized, or your story being told, or your name being remembered. It belongs to you. It has always belonged to you.

The work of ethics — the daily, unglamorous, endlessly necessary work — is simply this: to see the dignity in others that they may not currently see in themselves. To act accordingly. To refuse to participate in systems that deny it. To speak, gently and clearly, when silence becomes complicity.

This is the work. It is enough. It is everything.

posts.title: Letters to the Unseen
posts.author: ELGhazi
posts.excerpt: Prayer is not a transaction. It is not a petition submitted to a bureaucracy in the sky. It is, at its deepest, a conversation with the ground of your own being.
posts.content:
Prayer is not a transaction. It is not a petition submitted to a bureaucracy in the sky. It is, at its deepest, a conversation with the ground of your own being — an act of honest attention turned inward and then outward toward everything that is.

There is a particular quality of silence that comes after sincere prayer. Not the silence of emptiness, but the silence of arrival. As if you have, for a moment, stopped running from yourself and simply stood still in the presence of your own life.

We are so rarely fully present. Our minds live in yesterday's regrets or tomorrow's anxieties. We move through our days like ghosts haunting our own lives — visible but not quite there, going through the motions of living without fully inhabiting the moments as they come.

Prayer, at its best, is an interruption of that haunting. It is the practice of saying: I am here. This moment is real. I am more than my fears and my ambitions and my carefully maintained personas. There is something in me that is simply, quietly, alive.

The mystics of every tradition describe moments of what they variously call union, dissolution, fana, theosis — moments when the boundary between the self and the sacred becomes permeable. I do not know what to make of such accounts except to say that they point toward an experience of connection that seems to be one of the deepest human longings.

We want to be known. Fully known — not our public selves, not our best selves, but the complicated, contradictory, striving, failing, loving selves that we actually are. And we want that knowing to be met not with judgment but with something resembling grace.

Perhaps prayer is simply the practice of believing that such knowing is possible. That we are held, even when we cannot feel the holding. That the universe is not indifferent to our small and particular lives.

I cannot prove that. But I find I cannot stop believing it either.
